text: EXTEN, a village of Hesse-Cassel, in the circle of Schaumburg, 11/2 m. SSE of Rinteln, on the Exter. Pop. 777. It has considerable manufactories of cutlery and light iron goods.

EXTON, a parish in Rutlandshire, 5 m. ENE of Oakham. Area 4,860 acres. Pop. 282.—Also a p. in Somersetshire, 4 m. NNE of Dulverton. Area 3,870 acres. Pop. 380.—Also a p. in Hants, 5 m. NE of Bishop's Waltham. Area 2,210 acres. Pop. 282.

EXTREMOZ, a district and small town of Brazil, in the prov. of the Rio-Grande-del-Norte, about 12 m. from Natal, and 9 m. from the shore of the Atlantic, on Lake Guajiru. The district is bounded on the N by the Rio Massaranguape, and consists in its greater extent of low sandy tracts of land. Pop. 1,700.

EXUMA (GREAT), one of the Bahama islands, in N lat. 23° 30′, W long. 75° 50′. It is 30 m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth,—The Little E. is an islet off the SE extremity of the Great E., and to the E of Yuma or Long island.—There is also a chain of islands called the E. kays, which stretch along one edge of the Great Bahama bank, generally in a SW direction. They commence at Ship-Channel kay, 10 leagues ESE of the E end of New Providence, and extend to Great E., the harbour of which lies 50 leagues from New Providence.—The E. islands and kays have an area of about 220 sq. m., and a pop. of 1,400. Salt is their chief article of produce.
EXUPERY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. and 4 m. SE of Ussel. Pop. 1,425.

EYAFIALLA YOKUL, a mountain on the SW coast of Iceland, in N lat. 63° 37'. It is about 5,500 ft. in height, and is more than half covered with snow and ice. It is a volcanic mountain, and is supposed to have been in action in 1717 [Henderson], and was in violent action in 1821-2. The Godalands Yökul lies 12 m. to the NE, and the Myrdal's Yökul 13 m. E by S of this mountain.
EYAFIORD, a river, bay, and district, on the N coast of Iceland. The river, before falling into the bay, forms several beautiful islands, whence the name Eyafiord, i. e. 'Island-bay.'

EYAM, a parish in the co. of Derby, 5 m. E by N of Tideswell. It comprises the townships of E. and Woodland-E., and the hamlet of Foolow. Area 5,030 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,281; in 1831, 1,372; in 1841, 1,426. Pop. of the town in 1801, 817; in 1841, 951. In 1665, when the plague was raging in London, the infection was conveyed to this village, and carried off 259 of the inhabitants.—E.-dale is highly romantic and picturesque.
EYBAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 23 m. WSW of Saint Sebastian. Pop. 2,000. It has manufactories of fire-arms.
EYBENSCHUTZ. See EIBENSCHITZ.
EYBOULEUF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Saint-Leonard, 10 m. E of Limoges. Pop. 371.
EYBURIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Uzerche. Pop. 1,381.
EYDER. See EIDER.
EYDON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 9 m. SSW of Daventry. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 647.

EYE, a borough and parish in Suffolk, 20 m. N of Ipswich, and 895 m. NE by N of London, on a branch of the Waveney, and near the Eastern Counties railway. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. of p. in 1801, 1,734; in 1841, 2,493.—The town, which takes its name from being nearly surrounded by a rivulet, is situated nearly in the centre of the p. It is irregularly built, and has neither trade nor manufactures, though it is of some importance to the neighbourhood as a market-town. It sent 2 members to parliament, until deprived of one by the reform bill. By the parliamentary boundary bill, the ps. of Hoxne, Denham, Redlingfield, Occold, Thorndon, Braisworth, Yaxley, Thrandiston, Broome, and Oakley, are added, to form the new parliamentary borough, the pop. of which in 1831 was 7,347. The number of electors registered in 1837 was 328; in 1848, 330. The income of the borough in 1839 was £383.—Also a p. in Hereford, 3 m. NNE of Leominster. Area 4,530 acres. Pop. in 1801, 509; in 1841, 739.—Also a p. in the liberty of Peterborough, co. of Northampton, 3½ m. NE of Peterborough. Area 2,670 acres. Pop. in 1801, 501; in 1841, 1,359.—Also a liberty in the p. of Sonning, co. of Oxford, 5 m. S by W of Henley-upon-Thames. Area 2,550 acres. Pop. in 1801, 705; in 1841, 868.

EYE, a small river in Berwickshire. It rises among the Lammermoor-hills, in the p. of Cockburnspath; pursues a SE course over a distance of 11 m.; and then, making a sudden bend, flows 3\frac{1}{2} m. NE to the sea at Eyemouth.

EYEMOUTH, a small parish and village on the coast of Berwickshire. Pop. of p. in 1801, 899; in 1841, 1,401.—The ancient little seaport of E. lies at the mouth of the Eye, 6 m. N of Berwick. Its plan is altogether irregular, and, considering its size, is not a little intricate. Several modern and spacious erections, used as granaries, indicate the existence of important traffic with the surrounding agricultural country. The town is in fact the market for a somewhat extensive district, and the only seaport in Berwickshire. Even half-a-century ago 20,000 bolls annually, and in some years more than 40,000 bolls, were shipped here for Leith and other markets. The herring-fishery of E. was for many years so opulent and productive that 10,000 barrels were made up annually; but since 1820 it has greatly declined. Along with the remnant of this traffic, which sends its white herrings to Ireland or the Baltic, and its red or smoked herrings to England, a fishery of cod and haddock employs 9 or 10 boats, each manned by 6 men. "The harbour of E.," said the well-known Smeaton after professionally surveying it, "lies at the corner of a bay in which ships can work in and out at all times of the tide, or lie at anchor, secure from all winds except the N or NE." In all the space along the rugged and dangerous coast between the Forth and the Humber, no harbour except this is accessible in stormy weather. Vessels, therefore, which are arrested by contrary winds, or otherwise endangered in their transit, very numerously run to it for protection. The flow of the tide here, as generally in other harbours on the E coast, averages 10 ft. at neap-tides, and 16 ft. at spring-tides. The annual number of arrivals and departures of vesselsnot including any which run into the bay simply for shelter-is about 200. The cargoes outwards are EYR agricultural produce, fish, malt, and whisky; and those inwards are coals, slates, tiles, bricks, timber, rags, bones, and merchant goods.

EYEO, or KATUNGA, the cap. of the kingdom of Yarriba, in Sudan, in N lat. 8° 59', E long. 6° 12'. It is built on the sloping side and round the base of a small range of granite hills which, as it were, form the citadel of the town. A belt of thick plantation runs round the walls, which are of clay, and 20 ft. high. There are 10 gates in the walls, which are said to be 15 m. in circuit. The markets are wellsupplied with yams, corn, plantains, bananas, vegetable butter, sweetmeats, goats, fowls, sheep, lambs, cloth the manufacture of the country, and agricultural instruments.—Clapperton.
EYEOS. See Ayos.
EYERDIR. See EGERDER.
EYERIES, a village in the p. of Kilcatrine, co. Cork. Pop. 222.

EYERLANDT, a small island of Holland, about <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> m. in length, and a <math>\frac{1}{2}</math> m. wide, joined to the island of Texel by a bank of sand which is overflowed at high water.
EYETON. See AYTON.
EYFEL. See EIFEL.
EYFORD, a parish in Gloucestershire, 3 m. WSW of Stow-on-the-Wold. Area 1,380 acres. Pop. in 1831, 55; in 1841, 83.
EYGALIE'RES, a small town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Orgon, 12 m. E of Tarascon. Pop. 1,414. Fine marble is quarried here.
EYGUES, a river of France, which rises near Remusat, in the dep. of Drôme, and runs into the Rhone near Vaucluse, after a course of 60 m.
EYGUES. See AIGUES.
EYGURANDE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Correze, arrond. of Ussel. —The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1841, 5,890. -The town, 10 m. NE of Ussel, had a pop. of 921 in 1841.

EYGUYE'RES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, arrond. of Arles.—The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1841, 7,889.—The town is situated on an arm of the canal of Crapone, 17 m. E by S of Tarascon. Pop. 1,920. Oil and silk are grown in the vicinity.
EYKE, a parish in Suffolk, 3½ m. ENE of Woodbridge. Area 2,970 acres. Pop. 502.
EYLANDEN, one of the smaller Japanese islands, in N lat. 34° 45'.
EYLAU. See EILAU.
EYLE'S ISLAND, a small island in the Mergui archipelago, in N lat. 10° 46'.
EYLIAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Chignac. Pop. 1,068.
EYMET, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. 6,782 in 1841.—The town, situated on the Dropt, 9 m. S of Bergerac, had a pop. of 1,706.

EYMOUTIERS, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne.—The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1841, 14,376.—The town, situated on the l. bank of the Vienne, 18 m. E by S of Limoges, had a pop. of 3,491.
EYNAPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bijapur, in N lat. 16° 50'.
EYNDHOVEN. See EINDHOVEN.
EYNE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of E. Flanders, 12 m. N of Audenard. Pop. 2,540.
EYNESBURY, a parish of Huntingdonshire, S of St. Neot's. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 1,003.
EYNESFORD, a parish of Kent, 19 m. SE of London. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 1,313.
EYRAGUES, a commune and town of France, -in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, 8 m. ENE of Tarascon. Pop. 2,227.

EYRANSID, a lofty range in the Somali country of NE. Africa, supporting the E flank of the plateau of Jebel-Ahl-Ur-Singalli, at a distance of about 25 m. from the sea. From its summit, the traveller looks down a sheer precipice of 1,500 or 2,000 ft., and sees the villages of the Ahl-Ur-Singalli dotted along the sandy sea-coast 6,500 ft. below him. The summit is composed of tabular masses of limestone, covered with small nodules, and very cellular. The wild fig is thinly scattered over the ridge; the dragon's blood, ebony-tree, aloe, and cedar are plentiful. -Cruttenden.

EYRARBAKKI, a harbour on the S coast of Iceland, 3 m. E of the mouth of the Hvita, in N lat. 63° 52'. Its entrance is narrow and tortuous; and even when a vessel has got in, it must be made fast with four or five strong cables. The sea breaks completely over the beach here in winter.

EYRE CREEK, a stream of Central Australia, flowing from a salt lagune on the parallel of 25° S, and near the meridian of 138° 20' E, in a SSE direction. It has been traced nearly 60 m. through a flat flanked by high red sand hills.—Sturt.
EYRECOURT, a town of the p. of Dononaughta, co. Galway, 5 m. W by N of Banagher. Pop. 1,419.
EYSDEN, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 3 m. S of Maestricht, on the Meuse. Pop. 1,941.
EYSINES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Blanquefort. Pop. 2,000.
EYSSE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Lotet-Garonne, com. of Villeneuve-sur-Lot. Pop. 1,560. There is a large house-of-correction here. F

FAABERG, or FAUBERG, a parish and village of Norway, in the prov. of Aggerhuus, 80 m. NNW of Christiania, at the confluence of the Fare or Foere with the Lougen, 3 m. above the entrance of the latter into Lake Mjosen. Pop. 4,780.

FAABORG, a town of Denmark, in the stift of Fyen, bail. and 15 m. WNW of Svenborg, and 23 m. SSW of Odensee, on the Little Belt. Pop. 2,200. It is ill-built, and, with the exception of a church and an hospital, contains no buildings worthy of notice. It has a port, but it is small, shallow, and unsheltered. Its trade however, consisting in grain, alcohol, butter, cheese, wax, wool, and bones, is considerable. The surrounding district, although marshy, possesses considerable fertility.
FABARA, a small town of Spain, in the prov. of Saragossa, partido and 18 m. ENE of Alcaniz, on the I. bank of the Nonaspe, an affluent of the Ebro.
FABAS-DE-CONSERANS, a village of France, in the dep. of the Ariege, cant. of Saint-Croix-de- Volvestre, 10 m. NNW of Saint-Girons. Pop. 1,245.
FABBRICA, a town of the Pontifical states, in the del. and 12 m. ESE of Viterbo. Pop. 2,149.

FABBRICO, a village of the duchy of Modena, in the district and 16 m. NNE of Reggio. Pop. 1,920. III. THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF T FAB
EYTHORNE, a parish in Kent, 6 m. NW of Dover. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 423.
EYTON, a parish in Hereford, 2 m. NNW of Leominster. Area 850 acres. Pop. 177.—Also a p. in Salop, 3 m. N of Wellington. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 365.

EYVINDARHOLAR, a village on the S coast of Iceland, 11 m. E of the mouth of the Markarflijot. EYWANEE - KEY [ Fraser ], EYWANEKAIF [Holmes], a village of Persia, in the prov. of Irak- Ajemi, district of Veramine, or Vuromin, 20 m. WNW of Kishlak. It contains about 400 houses; and is surrounded by gardens famous for their figs.
EYWANOWITZ. See EWANOWITZ.
EYWORTH, a parish in Bedfordshire, 4 m. NE of Biggleswade. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 128.

EZA, or AISA, a village of Piedmont, in the district and 6 m. E by N of Nice. It is situated on the summit of a conical hill, crowned with the ruins of an old castle, and surrounded with deep ravines.
EZAGEN, a village of Fez, in Marocco, 60 m. S of Tetuan.
EZAWEN, a watering place for caravans in the desert of Sahara, 70 m. NW of Timbuctu.
EZCARAY, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. WSW of Logrono. Pop. 2,400. It has woollen manufactures.
EZCURRA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. NNW of Pampeluna.
EZDOUD. See ASHDOD.
EZETOVA, a river of Asiatic Russia, which runs into the estuary of the Obi, in N lat. 67° 50'.
EZQUIOGA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. SW of Saint-Sebastian.
EZY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Eure, 15 m. SE of Evreux. Pop. 860.
FABBRO, a town of the Pontifical states, in the deleg. of Viterbo. Pop. 773.
FABER'S MILLS, a village of Nelson co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 91 m. W of Richmond. Pop. in 1840 about 60.
FABIAN (SAINT), a town of the island of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, on the coast, on the E side of the Lingayen gulf.

FABIUS, a river of the United States, formed by the confluence of several streams which have their rise in the states of Iowa and Missouri, and which falls into the Mississippi, in Marion co., in the latter state, after a course of about 100 m.-Also a township of Onondaga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 120 m. W of Albany, watered by Toughnioga river. It has a hilly surface, but affords good pasturage. Pop. in 1840, 2,562.—Also a township of Marion co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. 1,340.
FABREZAN, a town of France, in the dep. of the Aude, cant. of Lezignan, 17 m. WSW of Narbonne, on the l. bank of the Orbieu, at the foot of a mountain. Pop. 1,160. It produces good wine.

FABRIANO, a district and town of the Pontifical states, in the deleg. and 29 m. W of Macerata, on the r. bank of the Giano, and at the foot of the Apennines. Pop. 6,619. It is the seat of the united bishopric of Fabriano and Matellica. It has manufactories of paper and parchment, and possesses a considerable trade in wool. Fairs are held three times a-year.
FABRIQUE-LA-NUEVA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Malaga and partido of Estepona, near the Guadiaro. Pop. 2,475.
FABRIZIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 19 m. SE of Monteleone. Pop. 400. It suffered much from an earthquake in 1783.
FACAO, or FALCAO, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, near the junction of the Almas with the Maranhão. See also Cunha.
FACARDINS (FOUR), a group of islets in the Low archipelago, South Pacific, to the N of Queen Charlotte's island, in S lat. 19° 20', and W long. 138° 24'.
FACATA, or FUKUKA, a town of Japan, in the island of Kiusiu, prov. of Tsikuzen, 36 m. WSW of Kokoura, on the NW coast of the island.
FACCOMBE, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 10 m. NNE of Andover. Area 3,170 acres. Pop. 276.
FACELY, a chapelry in the p. of Whorlton, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SSW of Stokesley. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 145.
FACH, a hamlet in the p. of Llanarth, Cardiganshire. Pop. 189.
FACHIN, a rivulet of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, an affluent of the Yonne.
FACHINGEN, a village of the duchy and 9 m. ENE of Nassau, on the l. bank of the Lahn. In the vicinity are the mineral springs of Birlenbach.
FACHWEN, a township in the p. of Llanfihangel, Montgomeryshire. Pop. 163.
FACILE, or Easy Harbour, an inlet of New Zealand, at the SW extremity of New Ulster, and enclosed on the W by Resolution island, in S lat. 45° 37′, and E long. 166° 30′.

FACONE, a lake of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Sagami, 54 m. WSW of Yedo. It is 6 m. in length, and about 5 m. in breadth, and gives rise to a small river of the same name, which flows into the gulf of Yedo. This lake is held sacred by the Japanese.
FACSET, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Krassova, 49 m. ENE of Temeswar, on the Boga.

FACTORY ISLAND, an island in the group of the Isles-de-Los, to the S of the embouchure of the Sangaria or Debrika, on the W coast of Africa, in S lat. 10° 33′, and E long. 14° 25′. It is 4½ m. in length.

FACTORYVILLE, a village of Tioga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 178 m. WSW of Albany, on Cayuta creek. Pop. in 1840, 350.—Also a village of Castleton township, Richmond co., in the state of New York, 153 m. S of Albany, on the N shore of Staaten island. Pop. 600.
FACZEBAJA, a village of Transylvania, in the comitat of Zarand. In the vicinity are mines of gold.
FADDILEY, a township in the p. of Acton, N. R. of Yorkshire, 43 m. WNW of Nantwich. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 320.
FADECHAMPS, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Mortier. Pop. 128.
FAD (LOCH), a lake in the island of Bute, 1 m. SSE of Rothesay, 5 m. long, and about half-a-mile in breadth.

FADIEFSKOY, an island in the Arctic ocean, in the archipelago of New Siberia, separated from Kotelnu island on the W by a shallow channel, and on the E from New Siberia by Blagovichensky or Annunciation strait, between 75° and 76° N lat., and between 141° and 146° E long. It is 96 m. in l of Swartsiolandet, in lake Mälar. FAE length from NW to SE, and about 40 m. in breadth. It runs out on the NW into a long narrow peninsula, and in the centre and W is covered with lofty mountains. Its surface is watered by a few small rivers. Bears, reindeer, and aquatic birds are its only inhabitants. This island was discovered in 1805 by Sannikov, and visited in 1821 by Lieutenant Anjou.
FADLA, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Nedjid, 200 m. NE of Mecca, on the caravan-route thence to Derayah.
FADMOOR, a township in the p. of Kirkby-Moorside, N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NE of Helmesley. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 156.
FADOAL, a small island in the Banda sea, Asiatic archipelago, to the W of Little Key, in S lat. 5° 55', and E long. 132° 22'.
FADOW, a town of Darfur, 100 m. SE of Kobbe, and 40 m. ESE of Ril.

FAECHDT, or FECHT, a river of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, which has its source in the Vosges, in the cant. and 7 m. N of Samarin, passes Munster, Turkeim, and Guemar, and below the latter town throws itself into the Ill, after a NE course of 33 m.
FAEDIS, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 8 m. NE of Udino, on the I. bank of the Griva. Pop. 2,839.
FAEGLOE, or Foglo, an island of the Baltic, in the group of the Aland islands, in N lat. 60°, and E long. 20° 25'. It has a parish church.

FAEMUND, a lake of Norway, in the laen of Hedemark, near the Swedish frontier. It is 37 m. in length from N to S, and 5 m. in average breadth. It discharges itself by a river of the same name on the SW into the Tryssild, one of the head-streams of the Klar, and by the Sor at its S extremity into the Dal.
FAENGOE, or FANGO, an island of the Baltic, near the E coast of Sweden, in N lat. 58° 15', and E long. 16° 55'.
FAENOL, a township in the p. of St. Asaph, Flintshire. Pop. 209.
FAENOL, or VAINOL, a township in the p. of Towyn, Merionethshire. Pop. 810.

FAENZA, a city of Italy, in the Pontifical states, leg. and 20 m. WSW of Ravenna, at the junction of the Zanelli canal, which joins the Po-di-Primaro canal at San Alberto, with the Lamone. Pop. 18,852. It is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Ravenna. Eustace describes this ancient town as spacious and well-built. It is surrounded by walls, and defended by a citadel. Its great square has a fine range of porticos on either side, and a Corinthian church belonging to the Dominicans. The cathedral, which is Gothic, stands in the great square, and is ornamented with a handsome steeple 5 stories high, with ballustrades. There is a fountain near the church, having a bason surrounded with four lions of brass. F. was once celebrated for its pottery ware, which bears the name of majolica. The manufacture still flourishes, but is now greatly surpassed by that of Germany and of England. The surrounding country is very rich and beautiful. Eustace observed in the vicinity of this city, a few traces of the pine groves which appear to have formed one of its distinguishing features in ancient times. It occupies the site of the ancient Faventia.
FAERDER, an island of Norway, on the SE coast, at the mouth of the gulf of Christiania, 18 m. E of Laurvig. It has a lighthouse in N lat. 59° 3' 35", and E long. 10° 37' 6".
FAERDREF, a township in the p. of Llandrillo, Merionethshire. Pop. 217.
FAERENTUNA, a village of Sweden, in the prefecture and 17 m. W of Stockholm, in the island
FAERILA, a village of Sweden, in the prefecture of Gefleborg, 99 m. NW of Gifle, on the Liusna.
FAEROE. See FAROE.
FAESJOEN, a lake of Sweden, in the prefecture of Œrebro, district and NW of Nora. It is 6 m. in length, and about 13 in average breadth.
FAF, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Walo, on the S side of the Senegal, in W long. 15° 58'.
FAGANS (Saint), a parish of Glamorganshire, 5 m. W of Cardiff, on the N bank of the Elwy. Pop. 424.

FAGARAS, or FOGARAS, an administrative district and town of Transylvania. The district, named in Hungarian, FAGARAS-VIDEKE, is bounded on the N by the circles of Schenk and Reps, and the comitat of Upper Weissenburg; on the E by the district of Cronstadt; on the S by Turkey in Europe; and on the W by the com. of Hermanstadt. Its length from E to W is 48 m., and its greatest breadth from N to S 24 m.; comprising a superficies of 650 sq. m. It is divided into 4 circles, and contains one town, viz., Fagaras, the capital, and 64 villages. Pop. in 1837, 59,905, chiefly Wallachians, Hungarians, and Saxons. It is generally mountainous, and is intersected in the S by a lofty chain of primitive mountains. The principal river is the Aluta. Rye, barley, oats, lint, and hemp form the chief articles of agricultural produce. The forests are extensive, and large numbers of cattle are reared on the pasture-land, which is excellent. Game and fish are abundant. Gold, silver, lead, and salt, are found in considerable quantities in the mountains.—The town, called also FAGRESCHMARKT, or HOLZDORF, is 45 m. NW of Cronstadt, and 40 m. E of Hermanstadt, on the l. bank of the Aluta, which is here crossed by a handsome bridge, 864 ft. in length. Pop. 4,860. It is well built, and has a handsome square, an old castle now used as a citadel, and several churches. It has a manufactory of leather, and conducts an active trade.
FAGERHULT, a town of Sweden, in the laen and 45 m. NW of Kalmar.
FAGGIONE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Otranto, district and 11 m. ESE of Tarento. Pop. 1,000.
FAGNANA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 8 m. WNW of Udine.

FAGNANO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the delegation and 23 m. NW of Milan, on the r. bank of the Olona, which is here crossed by a stone bridge. Pop. 1,800.—Also a town of the Pontifical states, in the delegation of Ravenna, on the Lamone, SW of Faenza.—Also a village in the delegation and 14 m. S of Verona, and district of Isola-della- Scala. In 1799 an engagement took place here between the French and Austrians.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 18 m. SE of Aquila, near the r. bank of the Alterno. Pop. 1,200.—Also a town in the prov. of Calabria- Citra, district and 25 m. NW of Cosenza. Pop. 2,000.
FAGNE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, cant. of Assesse. Pop. 306.—Also a commune in the prov. of Luxemburg, cant. of Havre. Pop. 112.
FAGNOLLE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur. Pop. 267. It has the ruins of a castle.
FAGNOULLE, a commune of Belgium, in the dep. of Liege, cant. of Villers-le-Bouillet. Pop. 174.

FAHAN (UPPER and Lower), two parishes of co. Donegal. The former lies on Lough Swilly. Area 24,782 acres. Pop. 5,823. The latter is <math>2\frac{1}{9}</math> m. S of Buncrana. Area 10,040 acres. Pop. 2,949.
FAHLEIM, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, 6 m. WSW of Gunzburg, on the r. bank of the Roth. Pop. 217.

FAHLUN (PROVINCE OF). See DALECARLIA. FAH FAHLUN, FALUN, or FALU, a town of Sweden, in the laen of Fahlun, in Dalecarlia, situated about 300 ft. above the level of the sea, between the two lakes of Run and Marpan, in N lat. 60° 35', E long. 15° 35', in a small plain surrounded on all sides by low hills. The town, which owes its existence to the celebrated copper-mines in its neighbourhood, is regularly built, and consists of several parallel streets crossed at right angles by others. The houses, which appear "like so many tarred boxes in the midst of a bleak and barren soil" [Clarke], are principally of wood and stone; and there are two churches built with brick, and one of them roofed with copper. Pop. in 1833, 4,379. The following account of the celebrated copper-mine of F. is abridged from the account given by Dr. Thomson in his travels in Sweden. The mine consisted of an immense cone of copper and iron pyrites, placed with its apex downwards. It has been wrought from time immemorial; and formerly with so little care, that about 150 years ago the whole works fell in, leaving a great hollow still very conspicuous as we approach the mine. Two large pillars, or rather hills of quartz, however, remained unmoved amidst the ruins, and may be still seen rising through the bottom of the great excavation. For many years galleries were driven through the old works, and the ore extracted in that manner; but the vast heap of ruins has been wrought out, and the miners are obliged to go deeper for the ore which at present they extract. The mine is about 200 fath. deep. The descent is by an easy winding staircase, without any other inconvenience than the fatigue of going down so many steps. The galleries are all spacious; none are less than 8 ft. in height, and some as high as 30 ft. There are about 600 workmen in the mine. The ore is extracted partly by the mallet, partly blasted by gunpowder. It is extremely poor, seldom yielding more than 1 per cent. of the copper; to the eye, indeed, it has nearly the appearance of iron pyrites. This pyrites is frequently mixed with beautiful crystals of actinolite; which give it when fresh broken a very splendid appearance. The ore is drawn up the perpendicular shafts; and all the machinery belonging to the mine is driven by water. The water that collects in the mine contains sulphate of iron in solution. As this water contains likewise a little sulphate of copper, it is pumped up, and made to run slowly through a pretty long trough containing pieces of old iron. By this contrivance the copper is precipitated, and being collected occasionally, is smelted. The water thus freed from copper, though it contains sulphate of iron, is much too weak to render it profitable to crystallize the salt by means of heat, but is concentrated by a very ingenious method borrowed from Germany. "More than 1,200 people have formerly been employed at once in this subterranean world, which, in its enormous labyrinth of passages, shafts, caverns, and halls, represents an excavated nether world,—the ideal of a mine-king's palace. It is said to demand more than 8 days to go through all its rooms, as far as the territory of Terra-Nova, and the region of Whereto, which extends 200 faths. deep below the bottom of the mine. The various and picturesque names of these rooms and halls, gathered out of all periods of history, out of the kingdoms of both fancy and reality, contribute to give to the place a romantic interest for the imagination. Almost all the kings and queens of Sweden have paid visits to this mine. Charles IX. was extremely attached to this mining country, was often residing here, called the mine 'Sweden's Fortune,' and desired that the great room might be called 'the Room of God's gifts.' His great son, Gustavus Adolphus, also exclaimed, as he stood in one of the rooms of the mine, when レー・ナリ・リリ カストスト ストドン トリー・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・アン・ the bright copper ore beamed from the walls, roof, and floor, 'Where is the monarch who has such a palace as that in which we now are?" The whole surface of the plain on which the mine is situated is strewed with immense round blocks of granite, quartz, felspar, hornblende, and chlorite slate; but not a single rock is to be seen in sitû, except the two pyramids of quartz formerly mentioned. But upon the higher grounds which surround F., especially to the W, rocks are found exposed in considerable quantity. The rock which environs F. is a particular kind of felspar. The rocks on the E side of the mine, at the distance of some miles, are gneiss. The vein-stones appear to consist chiefly of quartz.—F. is the oldest, and for many years it was the most productive, mine in Sweden. It is known to have been wrought since the year 1347. Nor is there any reason to believe the working of it began only then, though no authentic records go to a greater antiquity. In 1600 it yielded annually about 8,000,000 lbs. of copper, but for some time back the produce has greatly diminished; at present it does not exceed 400 tons per ann., and there is reason to expect that no great number of years will elapse before it ceases altogether to be productive. The copper-mine of F. is the property of a considerable number of individuals. When the ore is brought to the surface of the earth, it is divided into different portions according to the requisite shares of each individual. Every one is at liberty either to smelt his own ore in the way he thinks proper, or to dispose of it to those who are inclined to purchase it. From this method of proceeding it happens that there are many different smelting-houses, and that each house conducts its processes upon a small and economical scale.— Coxe's Travels.—Catteau, Voyage en Allemagne et en Suede, vol. ii. Paris, 1810. — Thomson's Travels through Sweden, chap. xii. p. 216; and Plates ix. and x. of that work, which exhibit a ground-plan of the copper-mine, and likewise a perpendicular section of it.—Clarke's Travels, vol. x.
FAHNERN (GROSS), a village of Saxe-Coburg- Gotha, 10 m. NE of Gotha, and 13 m. NW of Erfurt, at the foot of the Eckart's berg. Pop. 553.
FAHR, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle of Neuwied. Pop. 311.

FAHRAFELD, a village of Austria, in the district of Lower Austria, circle of Lower Wienerwald, on the Triesting, 23 m. SW of Vienna. It has manufactories of ice and of brass. In the vicinity is the castle of Neuhaus.
FAHRAG, or BAHARI, a town of Persia, in the prov. and about 70 m. SE of Yezd.
FAHRENBACH, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, N of Lohrbach. Pop. 649.
FAHRENHORST, a village of Hanover, in the principality of Luneburg, 19 m. ENE of Gifhorn. Pop. 270.
FAHRSUND, a town of Norway, in the dio. and 50 m. W of Christiansand, in the peninsula of Lister, near the shore of the North sea. Pop. 496. It has extensive fisheries.
FAHRWANGEN, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Aargau, to the E of the Haffwyler-See, 12 m. SE of Aarau. Pop. (Prot.) 708.

FAHRWASSER, or NEU FAHRWASSER, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Prussia, circle and 3 m. N of Dantzig, on the l. bank, and at the mouth of the W arm of the Vistula, opposite the fortress of Weichselmünde. Pop. 1,300. It forms properly a suburb of Dantzig, and is the port of that town.
FAICCHIO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, district and 8 m. SE of Piedimonte. Pop. 1,500. It has a church, and 2 convents.

FAIDO, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of | 169 m. NE of Harrisburg. ~ FAI Tessino, in the valley of Levantina, on the l. bank of the Tecino, at an alt. of 2,844 ft. above sea-level, and 16 m. NNW of Bellinzona. It is well-built, and has a convent of Capuchins. Pop. 615. FAIFO, Hue', or Haifo, a seaport town of Cochin-China, in the prov. of Quang-ngan, situated on the banks of an estuary communicating with Turan bay. The river which flows into the estuary was formerly navigable for large junks, but will now admit only vessels of 100 tons burthen. The junks lie at the distance of about 3 m. from the town, in another river communicating with that of Turan, and capable of receiving ships of 200 tons. The best anchorage is on the W side of the island of Cham-Calloa, or Calao-cham, in N lat. 15° 54', opposite the river, and about 3 leagues from the main. The town of F., which is about 10 m. from the sea, was once very large, and possessed a flourishing commerce. The houses are neatly built of stone and lime, and roofed with tiles; and the streets are well-paved. It was destroyed during the civil wars of the empire; but has recovered much of its former importance, and has a pop. of about 50,000, which is increased in the junk-season by at least 10,000, chiefly Chinese. The vessels which arrive at F. are principally Japanese, Chinese, and country-ships from India. The Chinese junks import alum, China-ware, tea, dried fruits, drugs, sandal-wood, linen cloth, false pearls, ornaments, pepper, dried blubber, dammer, ink, isinglass, sweetmeats, talc, lackered ware, and great quantities of tutenague. The Japanese import copper, besides several articles similar to those already enumerated. . The country-ships import European and Asiatic commodities, such as braziery, cutlery, cloths, guns and gunpowder, saltpetre, silver, clocks and watches, tobacco, opium, gold lace, brimstone, camblets, furs, glass ware, hardware, ironmongery, lead, looking - glasses, mathematical instruments, piece-goods, tin, sandal-wood, swords, shot, and vermilion. The exports to China are principally beetlenut, black wood, bullocks' bones, calavances, cardamoms, cotton, dried sea-snails, dried fish, drugs, pepper, Japan-wood, powder, agala-wood, beche-de-lamer, birds' nests, cassia, cloves and nutmegs, deer's sinews, mats of rattan, elephants' teeth and bones, fish glue, gamboge, coarse linen, wood for incense, seeds, skins of bullocks, deer, and elephants, sticklac, tin, wax, sugar, and sugar-candy. The exports to Japan are ebony, mammorak, pepper, fish-skins in great quantities, hare-skins, cow-hides, buffalohorns, white and dark sugar, Cambodia nuts, bark, drugs, and silk stuffs. The exports to India are principally sugar-candy and sugar. Various articles are also exported to Batavia, Manilla, and Siam.
FAILON, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, cant. of Barvaux-Condroz. Pop. 184.
FAILDE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, comarca and 30 m. WNW of Miranda.
FAILSWORTH, a township in the p. of Manchester, 4½ m. ENE of Manchester, intersected by the Rochdale canal. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 3,879.
FAINS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. and 2 m. N of Bar-le-Duc. Pop. 962. It has an hospital for incurables, and a cotton spinning-mill.
FAIOUM. See FAYOUM.

FAIRBURN, a township in the p. of Ledsham, W. R. of Yorkshire, 2½ m. NNW of Ferrybridge, intersected by the York and North Midland railway, which is here carried across the Air by a bridge. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 523.
FAIRDALE, a village of Rush township, Susquehanna co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S.,

FAIRFAX, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Vermont, 57 m. NW of Montpelier, watered by Lamoille river and its tributaries. It is level, and has a light, well-cultivated, fertile soil. Pop. in 1840, 1,918.—Also a county of the state of Virginia, comprising an area of 450 sq. m., bounded on the E by Potomac river and the district of Columbia, and watered by Ocoquan and Difficult creeks. It presents a hilly surface, and possesses little fertility. Pop. 9,370, of whom 5,469 were whites. Its capital, which bears the same name, is 121 m. N of Richmond, and contained, in 1840, 250 inhabitants. -Also a village and chief place of Culpepper co., in the same state, on the S bank of Mountain creek.
FAIRFAX (Mount), the S and most elevated part of Moresby's Flat-topped range, near Chapman river in W. Australia. Alt. 582 ft.

FAIRFIELD, a chapelry in the p. of Hope, Derbyshire, 1 m. ENE of Buxton. Pop. 599.—Also a parish in Kent, 61 m. WNW of New Romney. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 68.—Also a village of Lancashire, in the township of Droylesden, p. of Manchester. See Manchester. Pop. 900. — Also a village in the p. of Stogursey, Somerset, 83 m. WNW of Bridgewater, near the Bristol channel.

FAIRFIELD, a county of the state of Connecticut, U. S., comprising an area of 630 sq. m., extending 40 m. along Long Island sound, and watered by Housatonic river, which bounds it on the NE and E, and by Sangatuc, Norwalk, Mill, and Pequonuck rivers. It has a pleasantly-diversified surface, and a good and well-cultivated soil. It has many good harbours, and possesses considerable shipping in the coasting-trade, and fisheries. Pop. in 1840, 49,917. Its capitals are Fairfield and Danbury. The former, which is a port of entry as well as semi-cap. of the co., is 58 m. SW of Hartford, and 21 m. SSW of New Haven. Its surface is undulating, and the soil fertile and well-cultivated. Pop. 3,654. The village, consisting of a single street, contains about 100 dwellings.—Also a central county in the state of Ohio, comprising an area of 540 sq. m., lying at a considerable elevation between Muskingum and Scioto rivers, and drained by the head-waters of Hockhocking river. It contains a few conical hills, but is generally level and very fertile. Pop. 31,924. Its capital is Lancaster.—Also a central district of the state of South Carolina, containing a superficies of 796 sq. m., bordered on the NE by Wateree river, on the SW by Broad river, and intersected in the W by Little river, an affluent of the latter. Pop. 20,165. Its capital is Winnsboro.—Also a township of Somerset co., in the state of Maine, 22 m. N of Augusta, bordered on the N and E by Kennebec river. Pop. 2,198.—Also a township of Franklin co., in the state of Vermont, 56 m. NW of Montpelier, watered by branches of Missisque river. It has an irregular surface, but possesses considerable fertility. Pop. 2,448.—Also a township of Herkimer co., in the state of New York, 8 m. N of Little Falls, and 79 m. NW of Albany, drained by West Canada creek. It has a hilly surface, chiefly adapted to pasturage. Pop. 1,836.—Also a township of Cumberland co., in the state of New Jersey, 72 m. SW of Trenton, drained by Cohansey, Nantuxet, and Cedar creeks. It is level, and, with the exception of a marshy strip on Delaware bay, sandy. Pop. 1,935.—Also a village of Caldwell township, Essex co., in the same state, 11 m. NW of Newark, consisting, in 1840, of about 10 dwellings.-Also a village of Hamilton township, Adam's co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 43 m. SW of Harrisburg, on the S side of Middle creek.—Also a township of Crawford co., in the same state. Pop. 1,213.—Also a township of Westmoreland co., in the same state, in a FAI valley bounded on the NE by Conemaugh river, and watered by several of the affluents of that river. Pop. 2,035.—Also a township of Butler co., in the state of Ohio, containing Hamilton village, the capital of the co. Pop. 2,171.—Also a township of Columbiana co., in the same state. Pop. 181.—Also a township of Highland co., in the same state. Pop. 3,544.—Also a township of Tuscarowas co., in the same state. Pop. 866.—Also a township of Huron co., in the same state, 100 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 1,067.—Also a village of Green co., in the same state, 57 m. SW of Columbus, beautifully situated on the SE side of Mad river. Pop. 400.—Also a township of Madison co., in the same state. Pop. 505.—Also a village of Nelson co., in the state of Kentucky, 47 m. SW of Frankfort, on a branch of Salt river.—Also a village of Rockbridge co., in the state of Virginia, 23 m. SSW of Stanton. Pop. 100.—Also a village, capital of Wayne co., in the state of Illinois, 145 m. SE of Springfield, on the border of Hargrave's prairie. Pop. 150. Castor oil is extensively manufactured here.—Also a township of Lenawee co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 837.
